This is not a practice of visualization. The instruction is to receive, not to project.

"We're not projecting any kind of image, whether preconceived or spontaneously imagined. We're not imagining. We're just witnessing the light that appears to your eyes in the darkness."

Observe with fine attention, "observing the little grains of light in their various patternings, in the various tincturings they take on, in their waves and ripples, in their multi-dimensionality."

"That sense of spatial distance dissolves in the darkness and the vision might be happening in your retina, in your brain, in a disembodied mind. It doesn't matter. You're just meeting the vision of the light in the darkness."

## Darkness is Also a Kind of Light

"You'll find that there's light in the darkness. And in fact, it serves as a testimony to the fact that, you know, what we call darkness is just a certain portion of the spectrum of illumination in its various modes. Darkness is also a kind of light."

After the practice, do not rush on to the next thing. "Just see how has this act of attention affected me? What is my state of being now, having having done this?"

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Integrating Contemplation into a Busy Life
snippets.body:
# Integrating Contemplation into a Busy Life

## The Paradox of a Busy Life

In a life with no end to responsibilities, it is easy to feel too tired for even ten minutes of contemplation. Yet this exhaustion is precisely the reason it is so necessary.

"The question, especially in the West where life is so busy... is if contemplation, even only for 10 minutes in the evening, is not too much when we are tired."

"The answer is that for that very reason... contemplation is required more... Just because we're busy and we're tired, is that a reason not to give ourselves the chance to practice contemplation? On the contrary, one could see it as all the more reason for needing it."

## The Meal Analogy

A formal practice session is like eating a meal, while the spiritual nourishment sustains you throughout the entire day.

"We take our food at certain times every day. Yet all the time, even when we are sleeping, the food nourishes our body."

"The explicit practice of reciting the name and thinking of the name, that's like the meal, but then all through the day one is digesting it and it's feeding the cells and it's nourishing you. And that happens when the name continues to live in your consciousness, even if you're not reciting it, it's there. It's feeding your blood."

## The Goal is Integration, Not Seclusion

Contemplation is not meant to be a separate, isolated activity. Its purpose is to infuse and inspire every aspect of life.

"If contemplation does not develop in such a form that everything one does in life becomes a contemplation, then contemplation does not do a person any good."

"It is not the Sufi's idea to retire in seclusion or to sit silent all day. The idea is that by contemplation, one must be so inspired... that progress is attained in every aspect of life."

## Spiritual Food for Your Life's Mission

Instead of taking more of your time and energy, contemplation delivers fresh energy that empowers your purpose in the world.

"You might find that the wazifa actually, instead of just taking more of your time and energy, it it empowers you. It it delivers fresh energy that can empower your life's mission."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Enduring Nourishment of Spiritual Practice
snippets.body:
# The Enduring Nourishment of Spiritual Practice

## The Need for Practice in a Busy Life

"The question, especially in the West where life is so busy and where there is no end to responsibilities, is if contemplation, even only for 10 minutes in the evening, is not too much when we are tired."

The answer is that for this very reason, contemplation is required more, not less.

"Just because we're busy and we're tired, is that a reason not to give ourselves the chance to practice contemplation? On the contrary, one could see it as all the more reason for needing it. So busy, so preoccupied, so exhausted, a greatest need may indeed be letting our mind be with something, be with one theme, be with a particular quality, and let it wash over you, let it fill you, let it enliven you, rather than being so scattered constantly."

## The Meal and Its Digestion

Contemplation is like spiritual food, which nourishes the being long after the meal is over.

"We take our food at certain times every day. Yet all the time, even when we are sleeping, the food nourishes our body."

The fragmentary experiences of daily life are like fast food, quickly eaten. Contemplation offers slow-cooked nourishment, mindfully received.

"The explicit practice of reciting the name and thinking of the name, that's like the meal, but then all through the day one is digesting it and it's feeding the cells and it's nourishing you."

## What Truly Endures

"Yes, we are all very busy and yet so much of the busyness of what we're involved in is just going to turn to dust and what will be left of it? Very little."

"At the end of this life, what endures is what we've assimilated, what we've digested, what has really nourished our spiritual bones and that's what we've given care to, given love to."

The wazifa—our spiritual practice—is this essential food. Instead of just taking time and energy, it delivers fresh energy that can empower your life's mission.

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Ittisal Practice: Shifting from Body to Mind
snippets.body:
# The Ittisal Practice: Shifting from Body to Mind

## Stage One: The Sound "Ha" — Physical & Etheric Attunement

The first stage of the ittisal practice works with the syllable "ha".

"The ah. That you feel right here in the chest. Undoubtedly, it's vibrating the chest."

"There's a kind of clearing of the chest when you make that sound... it expands the lungs, quickens the circulation, wonderful sensation."

"As you're doing that, you're tuning into your embeddedness within the cosmos... You feel that interconnectedness animating and sustaining your body. And not only the physical body, the underlying etheric body, called the nasama."

## Stage Two: The Sound "Hu" — The Shift to the Mental

The practice evolves by adding the vowel "u" to create the sound "hu".

1.  After sounding "ha," introduce the vowel "u".
2.  Notice how the vibration shifts. "The ha is here, but if you say u, it's up here. It's higher up."
3.  Feel the energy lift from the chest, "through the throat, all the way up through the concavity of the mouth into the interior of the skull."
4.  Recognize the corresponding inner shift. "With that shift, there's a shift in identification from the physical and subtle physical or etheric to the mental, which is also called the astral."

## The Universal Mind (Nafs-i-kull)

This shift opens an awareness of the mind's place within a vaster consciousness.

"Now you are identifying your mental being, your psyche, as a fraction of the universal psyche, which is called the Nafs-i-kull."

"Our mind could never be what it is if it wasn't for our education and acculturation and ongoing communication with many other minds. It's a node within a vast web, a web of minds."

"As if your brain is one little neuron in a vast intergalactic brain. And whatever one is part of, one has access to."

"What I call 'my little mind' is mine, but also the universal mind is also my mind. It's my great mind. It's yours just as much as the little mind is yours."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Fiery Heart: Born of Sympathy and a Life Deeply Lived
snippets.body:
# The Fiery Heart: Born of Sympathy and a Life Deeply Lived

In the anatomy of the *Latif* (the subtle centers), the heart, or *Qalb*, is approached through the space of the physical heart.

"It's the subtle heart underlying the physical heart. And its flowering is a consequence of the maturation of the Nafs. The Qalb emerges out of the shell of the Nafs."

## Born of Sympathy

The blossoming of the heart begins when the personal self moves beyond its own boundaries into a state of fellow feeling.

"It's born of sympathy. When the Nafs isn't only competitive and oppositional, but begins to see not only its own self, but other selves, and feel not only its own suffering, but others' suffering, sympathy is born."

"When feeling bursts the bounds of the ego and becomes cosmic, becomes sympathetic, becomes open to the inspiration that fuels poetry and music, then the heart is at last blossoming."

## A Life Deeply Lived

The heart's character is forged through direct experience--it is not a center that can be cultivated by skimming the surface of life.

"The heart is born of a life deeply lived. It cannot come into its own when we skim the surface. It's born of pain and grace."

"There's passion in it, there's forgiveness, there's longing, a rawness that expresses an urgency which one can easily differentiate from the, let's say, cunning of the immature Nafs or the serene detachment of the Khafi."

## The Elemental Centers

Each subtle center has its own elemental nature, its own quality and mode of being.

"There's a fire in the heart. The Khafi is more airy. Nafs is more earthy. Heart is fiery and the soul is watery."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Luminous Soul: The Flowing Freshness of Ruh
snippets.body:
# The Luminous Soul: The Flowing Freshness of Ruh

"There's another complementary pair, pair of modes of being. And these are the organs of the heart and of the soul."

"In Murshid's teaching and in Suluk, soul means Ruh, not Nafs, Ruh. The heart is the Qalb, and the soul is the Ruh."

## The Fiery Heart (Qalb)

The heart is born from a life lived with depth, emerging from the shell of the Nafs through sympathy and fellow-feeling.

"The heart is born of a life deeply lived. It cannot come into its own when we skim the surface. It's born of pain and grace."

"There's passion in it, there's forgiveness, there's longing, a rawness that expresses an urgency... There's a fire in the heart."

## The Watery Soul (Ruh)

"The soul stands opposite to the heart... the soul is watery. It flows. Actually, the Persian synonym for Ruh is Ravan, which means flowing."

"Whereas the heart is raw, fiery, passionate, moved by intense love, the soul is luminous, watery, extremely gentle."

"The soul is fresh-sprung, you might say, from the source. There's a nascency in it, a fresh, sheer, simple immediacy."

## Strengthening the Heart

The freshness of the Ruh offers a counterpoint to the heart's intensity, bringing balance and healing.

"What strengthens the heart and stirs it from its anesthesia is the counterpoised Ruh with its fresh dawn light and its fresh dew water."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Subtle Centers as Portals to States of Being
snippets.body:
# The Subtle Centers as Portals to States of Being

## The Four Centers

The subtle centers, or latifas, are often understood in complementary pairs.

"The last two days we touched upon two latifas, first Nafs in the belly, and then the Khafi in the forehead, representing, uh, what you might call your personal self, your social self, your egoic self on the one hand, and your higher, mode of being, your wide vision, your deeper insight."

"Now there's another complementary pair, pair of modes of being. And these are the organs of the heart and of the soul... In Murshid's teaching and in Suluk, soul means Ruh, not Nafs, Ruh. The heart is the Qalb, and the soul is the Ruh."

These four centers correspond to the four elements:
*   **Nafs** (personal self) is earthy
*   **Khafi** (higher vision) is airy
*   **Qalb** (heart) is fiery
*   **Ruh** (soul) is watery

"Whereas the heart is raw, fiery, passionate, moved by intense love, the soul is, um, luminous, watery, extremely, um, gentle."

## Entry Points into States of Being

While the latifas are associated with specific places in the body--the belly, forehead, left chest, and right chest--these locations are best understood as portals.

"These modes of being are not restricted to one location, but these organs that we describe, these Latif are entry points into a certain, um, state of being."

"And actually there are even different systems of Latif and different teachers identify them in different places. So there's no ultimate objectivity about, um, any system, but, um, this is the way that's come down to us and if we look for these portals in these places, we'll find them."

## How to Open the Portals

We access these states of being through a gentle and respectful approach, using the breath as our medium.

"How do you open the portal in each place in the body? It's through, you knock on the door and you knock with the breath."

"And if you're going to the home of someone that you hold in high affection and esteem, you're not going to bang on the door, you're going to gently, gently knock and let the, you know, the cadence of your knock express something of your greeting to the one living inside. So that's going to show itself in the way in which we breathe in each space."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: A Contemplative Journey into a Fiery Landscape
snippets.body:
# A Contemplative Journey into a Fiery Landscape

A guided contemplation to "take a break from the humdrum existence in a virtual world of pure though exhilarating phantasm."

## A Note of Caution

This contemplation uses the imagery of fire. A gentle caution is advised for anyone who has experienced a related ordeal.

"I would advise caution... especially in this case where we're dealing with fire."

"Be easy on yourself. And I would say, you know, if you have any kind of background like that, this one just... be with us in spirit, but no need to join in this particular contemplation."

## The Contemplation

1.  Begin by "peering through a kaleidoscope," sparking a venture into worlds of light.
2.  "Imagine most realistically, spirals of light extending and closing in on you. Beings of light like a beacon at sea, random flashes and sparks bursting forth like fireworks."
3.  "Give yourself over to this enthralling effulgence until you feel a spillover into your clothes, in your aura, in your eyes."
4.  "Imagine that you are a firecracker exploding in innumerable sparks and colors, shapes and patterns, and then diffracting in a landscape of light."
5.  "Instead of sitting and enjoying the scene, you can step into this world of light, forging forward toward a horizon of light, getting brighter and more magnificent as you advance."
6.  Experience yourself soaring above the clouds at sunrise. Feel "the sheer ecstasy of buoyancy, of being part of this volatile skyscape."
7.  A realization dawns: "You realize that these are the blueprints of the forms of the world, sketched in light. Their architectonic seem like structures of temples of light, inhabited by beings of light."

## The Transformation

"There must be some reality to it because you can observe its effect upon you."

"As you gaze in the mirror, you cannot believe what you see. You are transformed beyond recognition, radiant, glowing."

"And if you offset your glance, you will discover behind the features of your face a countenance so beautiful you cannot believe it is you and think it is an angel revealing himself or herself to you."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Why the West Needs Contemplation More Than Ever
snippets.body:
# Why the West Needs Contemplation More Than Ever

## The Question of Busyness

A question often arises, especially in modern life where there is no end to responsibilities: when we are tired from a busy day, is adding even ten minutes of contemplation too much?

"The answer is that for that very reason, in the West, contemplation is required more than in the East, where everything, even the surroundings, is helpful to contemplation."

The fatigue and distraction of a hyper-industrialized world are not reasons to avoid contemplation--they are the very reasons it is so desperately needed.

"So busy, so preoccupied, so exhausted, a greatest need may indeed be letting our mind be with something, be with one theme, be with a particular quality, and let it wash over you, let it fill you, let it enliven you, rather than being so scattered constantly."

## Contemplation as Nourishment

For the practice to be effective, it cannot be isolated from the rest of life. It must become a source of nourishment that sustains you throughout the day.

"A person who gives 10 or 20 minutes to contemplation every evening and forgets it all day will not derive any benefit."

"We take our food at certain times every day. Yet all the time, even when we are sleeping, the food nourishes our body."

The fragmentary way we experience life makes it difficult to assimilate the divine nature within our experiences. Contemplation provides a way to slow down and receive deep nourishment.

"It's like slow-cooked food, eaten mindfully, digestion is incomparably better than fast food quickly eaten."

## The True Field of Practice

The goal is not to retire into seclusion, but to bring the fruits of contemplation into every aspect of existence.

"By contemplation, one must be so inspired in study and in aspiration that progress is attained in every aspect of life."

"Concentration and contemplation are great things, but no contemplation is greater than the life we have about us every day."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Practice of Shughli Mahtabi and Light in Darkness
snippets.body:
# Light, Darkness, and the Meeting of Opposites

We must be careful not to fall into a false dichotomy, thinking that light is good, benevolent, and welcome, while "darkness by contrast is something opposed to light, something to be shunned, something to be avoided or overcome."

## The Coincidentia Oppositorum

"Whenever we meet with a polarity in creation and we affirm only one pole, there's a danger of one-sidedness."

The poles are not truly separate. "In fact, the opposite poles have something of each other in each and they meet in a spiritual marriage. The *coincidentia oppositorum*."

"And in fact, the great Sufi al-Kharraz said to look for God precisely where the opposites meet."

## The Lesson of Shughli Mahtabi

This principle is revealed in the lunar practice, *shughli mahtabi*. While the moon can sometimes be seen in the daytime, it is at night that it becomes most visible.

"And this is especially true of the stars... We only see the stars in the black night sky."

"So darkness is showing us something. The veiling of the light of the sun enables other manifestations of light to be revealed."

Instead of thinking of darkness as the opposite of light, we begin to discover different modalities.

"One finds that by filtering out the most obvious and most glaring frequencies, the result is not the dispelling of luminosity, but the discovery of finer transmissions of light."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Upcoming Wazifa of the Seven Leading Names
snippets.body:
# The Upcoming Wazifa of the Seven Leading Names

## Your Spiritual Food

"So next time we meet, God willing, each of you will receive one of the seven leading names as your wazifa to do for a period of time and that will be your spiritual food."

This practice is an invitation to give yourself the time to focus on what endures beyond worldly activity.

"Yes, we are all very busy and yet so much of the busyness of what we're involved in is just going to turn to dust and what will be left of it? Very little."

"At the end of this life, what endures is what we've assimilated, what we've digested, what has really nourished our spiritual bones and that's what we've given care to, given love to."

## A Source of Energy, Not a Burden

While many of us are busy with honorable missions and offerings of service, this practice is not meant to be another exhausting task.

"You might find that the wazifa actually, instead of just taking more of your time and energy, it empowers you."

"It delivers fresh energy that can empower your life's mission."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Watery Soul (Ruh) and its Cognates
snippets.body:
# The Watery Soul (Ruh)

The soul, or Ruh, is one of four subtle centers, each with its own elemental quality. Where the Nafs is earthy, the Khafi is airy, and the heart is fiery--the soul is watery.

"Heart is fiery and the soul is watery. It flows."

## The Flowing Nature

"Actually, the Persian synonym for Ruh is Ravan, which means flowing."

This quality is also connected to the essence of life itself, reflected in the roots of words that signify spirit.

"Atman, in German you say atem, the breath, pure breath."

## Opposite the Fiery Heart

The watery soul stands in a complementary relationship to the fiery heart (Qalb).

"The soul stands opposite to the heart, whereas the heart is raw, fiery, passionate, moved by intense love. The soul is luminous, watery, extremely gentle..."

## A Fresh Immediacy

The soul possesses a unique quality of nascency, of being newly born from the source. It is distinct from the raw passion of the heart and the sober wisdom of the Khafi.

"The soul is fresh-sprung, you might say, from the source. There's a nascency in it, a fresh, sheer, simple immediacy, whereas the Khafi represents the sobriety that comes after intoxication."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Embracing Pain to Open the Heart
snippets.body:
# Embracing Pain to Open the Heart

## The Strategy of Avoidance

"The heart carries your store of emotions and that may be why so often the heart is closed down because there's too much to come to terms with there."

The protective impulse is "to shut it down and bury it. But then things go very wrong."

"By refusing pain, one dooms oneself to suffering."

## The Universal Gateway

"Sooner or later the time will come when it will be necessary to accept the pain in the heart, allow it, sense it."

This personal pain becomes a portal to a vaster reality.

"Through that pain, sense the pain that is in all of creaturehood, and in the same flash encounter the joy that is equally omnipresent through a mood of deep solidarity with all of the beings swept along by waves of becoming."

## Awakening the Heart

"The fact is what strengthens the heart and stirs it from its anesthesia is the counterpoised Ruh with its fresh dawn light and its fresh dew water."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Closing Prayers: Salat and Khatum
snippets.body:
# The Closing Prayers: Salat and Khatum

## Salat

"Most gracious sovereign, messenger, Messiah, savior of humanity, we greet thee with all humility."

"Thou art the first cause and the last effect, the divine light and the spirit of guidance, Alpha and Omega."

"Thy light is in all forms, thy love in all beings."

"Allow us to recognize thee in all thy holy names and forms. As in Sita and Rama, as in Radha and Krishna... as in Mary and Jesus, Khadija and Muhammad, and in many other names, holy names and forms known and unknown to the world."

"May the message of God reach far and wide, illuminating and making the whole humanity as in a single family in the parenthood of God. Amen."

## Khatum

"Thou who art the perfection of love, harmony, and beauty, the sovereign of heaven and earth, open our hearts that we may hear thy voice, which constantly cometh from within."

"Disclose to us thy divine light, which is hidden in our souls, that we may know and understand life better."

"Most merciful and compassionate God, give us thy great goodness, teach us thy loving forgiveness, raise us above the distinctions and differences which divide us. Send us the peace of thy divine spirit and unite us all in thy perfect being. Amen."

## Final Blessing

"May your heart be filled with heavenly joy. May your soul be illumined with divine light. May your spirit unfold the divine message. May you go on in the spiritual path. May God's peace abide with you forever and evermore. Amen."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Journey is in Each Step
snippets.body:
# The Journey is in Each Step

## The Destination is Present in the Step

We are travelers in a caravan, "finding our footing on the path that can only be taken one step at a time." This requires a reorientation of our perspective away from a distant goal and toward the reality immanent in the process itself.

"Somehow the whole journey is present in each step. The destination is already right here with us each step along the way."

## Realizing Your Divine Inheritance

The spiritual path is not about acquiring something new, but about realizing what is already within you.

"The whole of the path consists of this, not obtaining something external, but activating and realizing and integrating what is already one's own. Your birthright, your divinely appointed nature."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Lataif as Divine Inheritance
snippets.body:
# The Lataif as Divine Inheritance

## Activating What Is Already One's Own

"The whole of the path consists of this, not obtaining something external, but activating and realizing and integrating what is already one's own. Your birthright, your divinely appointed nature."

The Lataif--the subtle centers of our being--are not new knowledge to be acquired, but dormant faculties to be awakened. "Murshid calls it a divine inheritance."

## The Treasure in the Wall -- An Analogy

The Quran tells a story of Khidr and Moses arriving in a village where the people were unfriendly. Despite their rudeness, Khidr stopped to rebuild a wall that had fallen down.

"Later on, Khidr explained that it was because in the wall, a treasure was hidden, and that treasure was the inheritance of some orphans of the town."

"Because the people of the town were so ill-dispositioned, if they had seen the treasure before the orphans had grown to the age when they could claim it, the town's people would have taken it for themselves. Something was hidden for the children that would have been discovered by them and claimed by them when the time was right."

## Receiving Your Inheritance

"And so it is that in this universe there are inheritances that are meant for souls, meant for discovery by them when the time is right. When we are ready for them, when we have the means to use them."

"There is a knowledge that it's already your own, it's your inheritance, but it only reaches you when the time is right. So the teachings on the Lataif are like this."

"The Lataif are the limbs and organs of our own bodies, they're already ours. But the introduction to them... comes when we are ready."

## The Meeting of Seeker and Lineage

"Readiness comes when the seeker's motivation and dedication is clear, and when the lineage of those who came before is ready to meet the seeker in that intention. And there's a mutual joy in the in the meeting and in the transmission."

"It was meant to be received and it was meant to be passed down."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Contemplating the Ruh: The Light of Vision
snippets.body:
# Contemplating the Ruh — The Light of Vision

A practice for contemplating the Ruh, the subtle center on the right side of the chest, by cultivating spaciousness and embodying its light as the very substance of vision.

## The Practice of Preparation

1. Turn your attention to the right side of the chest. "Let the weight come off your shoulders. Let your chest open."
2. Create a feeling of permeability and receptivity. "Let your skin become translucent. The hard edges of your being soften."
3. Allow this softening to create an inner atmosphere. "And with that softening comes a sense of expansion, spaciousness, lightness of being."

## Evoking the Sensibility

"Sometimes an evocation of celestial grace and subtlety will help orient you to what is there."

These images can help conjure the sensibility of the Ruh:
*   "I envision a swan gliding."
*   "Or the crescent of the moon, a lake shimmering at night under the moon."
*   "A gossamer elegance."
*   "Or you might think of entering a crystal cavern or moving in a landscape of ice and snow."

## The Reversal of Perception

Once you sense the quality of the Ruh, you can let go of the images and give yourself over to its character.

"The light of the Ruh... might at first appear as something that you are encountering and contemplating. But more and more a reversal takes place."

"And now it isn't that you are looking upon the light of the Ruh, but rather the light of the Ruh is your vision. It's the substance of your vision. You don't see it, but you see by it."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Fiery Sun of the Heart
snippets.body:
# The Fiery Sun of the Heart

A contemplation on the Qalb--the heart center on the left side of the chest, characterized by the fiery blaze of the sun.

## Accessing the Heart's Light

"Spaciousness still holds the key."

To access this center, feel into the space within the physical body, allowing the subtle light to emerge from within that spaciousness.

"The space between the ribs, the space between the tissues of the organ of the heart, between cells, between molecules, between atoms."

"In the space, the light of the heart wells up."

## The Red Sun of the Qalb

This center is evoked by symbols of vibrant, active, and potent life.

"The red rose with its pungent fragrance and its bright vermilion hue and its sharp thorns."

It stands in dynamic contrast to the Ruh, the center on the right side of the chest.

"The cool passivity of the moon characterized the the Ruh. The fiery blaze of the sun characterizes the Qalb. It's the red sun opposite the white moon."

## The Practice -- Let Thy Sun Shine

"The sun of the heart is an atom of the divine sun."

Connect the personal heart-fire to its universal source with this breath practice from Hazrat Inayat Khan:

1. On the in-breath, silently repeat: *let thy sun*
2. On the out-breath, silently repeat: *shine in my heart*

"And so we say with Murshid, inhaling, let thy sun, exhaling, shine in my heart."

"And then let the words dissolve away, but stay with the experience."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Divine Senses: As-Sami' and Al-Basir
snippets.body:
# The Divine Senses -- As-Sami' and Al-Basir

The seven "mother names" of God include a pair corresponding to our most predominant senses, hearing and seeing. These are not just human faculties, but cosmic principles.

"In the elaboration of the cosmos from the eternal source, in the course of creation, these two senses correspond to the most foundational developments in the crystallization of manifestation: vibration, corresponding with hearing, and light, corresponding with seeing."

## From Power to Perception

The cosmos unfolds from raw power toward refined, loving intentionality--a process that necessitates the ability to perceive.

"The sheer intense willfulness of Qudrat, the power of Al-Qadir, gave way to the intentionality of Al-Murid."

"The discernment of possibilities, the pursuit of those possibilities, the construction of a whole universal architecture becoming increasingly complex, refined, organically interconnected, a process of tremendous intricacy, which bespeaks meaningfulness of an almost unfathomable degree."

## As-Sami' -- The All-Hearing

What emerges from love's quest? The need for true responsiveness. Acquisitive, projective love is immature; what is needed is the capacity to truly receive the other.

"What is needed is the ability to listen, and not only hear what you expect you're going to hear... Listening would require really being changed by what you hear, being ready to be surprised."

This deep listening attunes us to a cosmic symphony.

"When one listens very attentively, not only with the mind but with the heart, you find yourself in the middle of a planetary symphony, the music of creation that communicates the emotion of the creator."

Ultimately, our hearing is a vessel for a greater hearing. "Everything is heard by the divine audition, and you're simply participating in that endless listening."

## Al-Basir -- The All-Seeing

"Very close to As-Sami', the hearer, is Al-Basir, the seer. Hearing comes first because vibration arises before light. Light is an after-effect of vibration."

Like listening, true seeing goes beyond the surface to perceive the soul.

"This is the deeper seeing beyond surfaces, a kind of x-ray vision, seeing into minds, into hearts, into souls."

Our vision is a conduit for the divine vision.

"The Quran says regarding Allah, vision doesn't perceive Allah. Allah perceives vision."

"Even as we look for God, God is looking out through these searching eyes. And when we take heed of this, our glance is illuminated. We see better when we are not seeing for a self that begins and ends. Our seeing is given over to the divine sight."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Art of Deep Listening
snippets.body:
# The Art of Deep Listening

## From Acquisitive to Mature Love

Love that is merely acquisitive and based on projection will never result in satisfaction. Mature love requires a deeper faculty.

"Love which is just acquisitive is very immature. It it's a kind of consumerism. One is trying to acquire an object."

"The love that is mature is a love that encompasses this reality of not a subject meeting an object, but a subject meeting a subject. And how each of them contains mystery, each of them possesses an infinite depth."

## The Practice of Listening

The key to this maturity is the ability to listen--not just to what you expect, but with a readiness to be transformed by what you hear.

"What is needed is the ability to listen, and not only hear what you expect you're going to hear, which is all too commonly the way of listening... Listening but just hearing what you expect, what your prejudice tells you you're going to hear."

"No, listening would require really being changed by what you hear, being ready to be surprised, not only listening to the words, listening to the cadence of the voice, the tone of the voice, what the person is saying, but also what's behind what the person is saying. Listening is a very deep art."

## The Planetary Symphony

This deep listening can be extended beyond human conversation to the entire natural world.

"Listening to the voices of the birds in the morning, listening to the rustling of the leaves in the wind, the lapping of the waves, the crack of thunder."

"When one listens very attentively, not only with the mind but with the heart, you find yourself in the middle of a planetary symphony, the music of creation that communicates the emotion of the creator."

## The Divine Listener

Ultimately, our own listening is a participation in the divine audition.

"You are aware of God as the listener. So your hearing is simply a vessel for the divine hearing. You don't claim it as your own."

"Everything is heard by the divine audition, and you're simply participating in that endless listening."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: God is the Listener
snippets.body:
# God is the Listener

## The Art of Deep Listening

Mature love requires the ability to listen--a practice that goes beyond merely hearing what you expect to hear.

"Listening would require really being changed by what you hear, being ready to be surprised, not only listening to the words, listening to the cadence of the voice, the tone of the voice, what the person is saying, but also what's behind what the person is saying. Listening is a very deep art."

When we listen with the heart, we find the divine voice communicating through all sounds, from the natural world to the industrial city.

"When one listens very attentively, not only with the mind but with the heart, you find yourself in the middle of a planetary symphony, the music of creation that communicates the emotion of the creator."

"Listen to it and listen to what is in the depths of it, listen to what's behind it. And you find that the divine voice is everywhere."

## A Shift in Identity

The practice of listening has two stages. The first is honing your own listening. The second is a radical shift in identity.

"So first you're you're listening to sound that you know reaches you ultimately from the divine source, and you're honing your listening. But secondly, you are aware of God as the listener."

In this realization, your personal hearing becomes a vessel for the divine hearing. You do not claim it as your own.

"Everything is heard by the divine audition, and you're simply participating in that endless listening."

## Primal Memories of Audition

This deep listening can unlock primal memories, connecting you to the very source of sound and being.

"You might remember still earlier moments of audition when you were in the womb, listening to the mother's heartbeat. You might remember when it was music that led you into the womb to begin with. Your soul ushered down through the planes, through an aisle of resonant sound."

"You may even recall somewhere deep, deep down in your being, the very first encounter with the divine voice, the voice of which this whole creation is made."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Deeper Seeing of Al-Basir
snippets.body:
# The Deeper Seeing of Al-Basir

## Cosmic Sequence
"Very close to As-Sami', the hearer, is Al-Basir, the seer. Hearing comes first because vibration arises before light. Light is an after-effect of vibration."

The evolution of our senses is an elaboration of love's need for responsiveness. As our capacity for love grows, the heart develops its own faculties of perception.

## The Heart's Eyes
"You might say your heart is growing ears and also eyes. Not only the eyes to see, let's say the obviously attractive contours which appear in this world, but eyes to see into other pairs of eyes."

"This is the deeper seeing beyond surfaces, a kind of x-ray vision, seeing into minds, into hearts, into souls."

## The Divine Glance
"The Quran says regarding Allah, vision doesn't perceive Allah. Allah perceives vision."

"So we're aware of our glance as a conduit for the divine glance. Even as we look for God, God is looking out through these searching eyes."

"And when we take heed of this, our glance is illuminated. We see better when we are not seeing for a self that begins and ends. Our seeing is given over to the divine sight."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Love's Quest for Responsiveness
snippets.body:
# Love's Quest for Responsiveness

## Immature, Acquisitive Love

"Love which is just acquisitive is very immature. It it's a kind of consumerism. One is trying to acquire an object."

This approach of projecting one's own desires and expectations onto another will never lead to fulfillment.

"To merely project one's expectations, project one's desires is is incomplete and will never result in satisfaction."

## Mature Love: Subject to Subject

"But another person is not an object, another person is also a subject, just as each of us is a subject. And not only people are subjects, everything is a subject. Animals, plants, everything."

"The love that is mature is a love that encompasses this reality of not a subject meeting an object, but a subject meeting a subject."

In this meeting, we encounter the true nature of the other--a being with its own unfathomable depth.

"Each of them contains mystery, each of them possesses an infinite depth leading into the deepest heart of being."

## The Art of Deep Listening

To move from acquisitive love to mature love requires responsiveness--the ability to truly listen.

"What is needed is the ability to listen, and not only hear what you expect you're going to hear, which is all too commonly the way of listening, only half listening."

"No, listening would require really being changed by what you hear, being ready to be surprised... not only listening to the words, listening to the cadence of the voice, the tone of the voice, what the person is saying, but also what's behind what the person is saying. Listening is a very deep art."

"When one listens very attentively, not only with the mind but with the heart, you find yourself in the middle of a planetary symphony, the music of creation that communicates the emotion of the creator."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Intoning the Names of Hearing and Seeing
snippets.body:
# Intoning the Names of Hearing and Seeing

## Divine Hearing and Seeing

"You are aware of God as the listener. So your hearing is simply a vessel for the divine hearing. You don't claim it as your own."

"The Quran says regarding Allah, vision doesn't perceive Allah. Allah perceives vision."

"Even as we look for God, God is looking out through these searching eyes."

"When we take heed of this, our glance is illuminated. We see better when we are not seeing for a self that begins and ends. Our seeing is given over to the divine sight."

## The Practice of Intonation

A practice to experience the reality of As-Sami' (the Hearer) and Al-Basir (the Seer).

1. While intoning the name *As-Sami'*, give your attention to your ears.
2. While intoning the name *Al-Basir*, give your attention to your eyes.
3. In both movements of the practice, "give them over to the divine."

"Let God be the heard and the hearer. Let God be the seen and the seer."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Progression of the Lataif: From Belly to Chest
snippets.body:
# Progression of the Lataif — From Belly to Chest

## From Earth to Heaven

"We began with the center at the belly, and then went on to the center in the forehead, from the Nafs to the Khafi..."

## Into the Heart

"...and then on to the centers of the left and right side of the chest, which are solar and lunar in nature."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Lost and Found: The Timely Appearance of Sacred Knowledge
snippets.body:
# Lost and Found: The Timely Appearance of Sacred Knowledge

## The Reappearance of Treasure

"Even to this day, there are things that Murshid wrote, writings of Pirzadi Shahida Noor, which have disappeared only to suddenly appear. And whenever these treasures appear, I know it is because they are now given to us, we're meant to have them, to learn from them."

## Your Own Inheritance

"And that is true in the in the life of each of us, there is a knowledge that it's already your own, it's your inheritance, but it only reaches you when the time is right."

## The Right Time for the Lataif

"So the teachings on the Lataif are like this. The Lataif are the limbs and organs of our own bodies, they're already ours. But the introduction to them in a world in which so much knowledge is forgotten, so much of our true nature is veiled, the introduction comes when we are ready."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Seven Mother Names: A Review of the Divine Attributes
snippets.body:
# The Seven Mother Names: A Review of the Divine Attributes

## The Divine Unfolding

"Now, alongside the seven Lataif, we have also been gradually getting to know seven divine names, seven leading names, seven mother names."

"And these have been thus far, Al-Hayy, the living, Al-'Alim, the knowing, Al-Qadir, the powerful, and Al-Murid, the willing or the desiring."

## The Senses of the Cosmos

The path now leads to a pair of attributes that govern perception and responsiveness: As-Sami' (The All-Hearing) and Al-Basir (The All-Seeing).

"They correspond to the senses which are the most explicit ones in our experience."

"In the elaboration of the cosmos from the eternal source, in the course of creation, these two senses correspond to the most foundational developments in the crystallization of manifestation: vibration, corresponding with hearing, and light, corresponding with seeing."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Gaze of the Heart and Limbic Resonance
snippets.body:
# The Gaze of the Heart and Limbic Resonance

## The Heart Grows Eyes

As your capacity for love grows, your heart develops its own senses.

"You might say your heart is growing ears and also eyes. Not only the eyes to see, let's say the obviously attractive contours which appear in this world, but eyes to see into other pairs of eyes."

## Limbic Resonance and Deeper Seeing

This meeting of souls has a physiological basis--a deep resonance that goes beyond surface appearances.

"This meeting of glances brings about what scientists sometimes call limbic resonance. You see into the heart of a person, and your own heart is affected by the encounter."

"This is the deeper seeing beyond surfaces, a kind of x-ray vision, seeing into minds, into hearts, into souls."

## The Divine Glance

Our personal sight is a conduit for a perception that is vaster than our own.

"The Quran says regarding Allah, vision doesn't perceive Allah. Allah perceives vision."

"So we're aware of our glance as a conduit for the divine glance. Even as we look for God, God is looking out through these searching eyes."

When we realize this, our perception is transformed. "Our seeing is given over to the divine sight."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Copper Rule of Willing Help
snippets.body:
# The Copper Rule of Willing Help

## The Rule

"My conscientious self, extend your help willingly to those in need."

The rules always begin with the phrase "My conscientious self," which is a crucial reminder of their purpose.

"It means that we're speaking to ourselves. We're not, um, pointing a finger at anyone else, and we're not going to use these rules to admonish anyone. Everyone is responsible for themself and accountable to their own conscience."

"We're speaking to that dimension of ourself which is awake and aware, conscientious, mindful, bringing it to the fore."

## Mutual Support

"You can imagine a world in which when people found themselves in real need, others recognized the fact and were there to help. It could happen to any of us at any time."

Life is a series of changing roles. In one season we may find ourselves in need, and in another, we may be in a position to offer help.

"If we're all committed to the principle, we can all find our way through together. But if everyone is out for themself, in the end, everyone loses."

## The Story of the Four Friends

A Jataka tale illustrates this principle. There were four friends living in a forest: a hare, a jackal, an otter, and a monkey. The hare proposed that on the next holy day, they should fast and offer whatever food they found to someone who might be hungry. The friends all agreed.

The next morning, the jackal found a jar of milk and a piece of meat. The otter found fish buried in the sand. The monkey plucked ripe mangoes from a tree. Each of them, while willing to give their findings away, secretly hoped they would get to keep the meal for themselves.

The hare, however, was in a state of apprehension. "I eat grass, but no one else eats grass." What could he possibly offer?

Meanwhile, a fairy, the guardian spirit of the forest, had overheard their pledge and decided to test their resolve. Disguised as an emaciated old woman, she approached each of them in turn. The jackal, the otter, and the monkey all willingly offered up their food when she asked, but each time, she declined and moved on.

Finally, she came to the hare, who had nothing to give. He was utterly distraught until an idea flashed in his mind.

"I have nothing to give, but let me just give myself."

He asked the old woman if they could make a fire. She caused a fire to leap up.

"And the hare smiled and without any hesitation, leaped into the fire to be cooked, to be offered as food for the crone."

But something strange occurred--the flames were cool. The fairy revealed her true form and said, "Oh, hare, I never meant to eat you. I was just playing a game, but your heart is so pure, so generous, so kind."

Then the fairy drew energy from a nearby hill, lifted her wand to the moon, and inscribed the hare's portrait upon it.

"And that's why when you go out to do your Shughl-i-Mahtabi at night on a clear night and you look up at the moon, you just might see a hare gazing down at you."

## The Meaning

"A story like this can say many things. Of course, it's a celebration of generosity and particularly the willingness to help someone in their hour of need, even if it means personal sacrifice."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Jataka Tale of the Four Friends
snippets.body:
# The Jataka Tale of the Four Friends

"There's a story that illustrates this in a moving way. And it's one of the Jataka tales... retold by Pirzadi Shahida Noor."

## The Pledge

"There were four friends, a hare, and a jackal, and an otter, and a monkey. And they lived in the forest."

One day, the hare reminded them that the following day was a holy day. "And so the hare said, why don't we do something different? Instead of eating the tasty things we find as usual, why don't we fast? And whatever we find, we'll offer it to someone who may be hungry."

The friends all agreed to this and went their ways.

## The Findings

The next morning, the jackal found a jar of milk and a piece of meat. "And he called out, does this milk and meat belong to anyone? And there was no one there, so he thought, might as well be mine... if anyone needs it, I'll give it to them, but otherwise, I'll have a delicious meal."

The otter found fish buried in the sand. "So the otter said, do these fish belong to anyone? ...if no one asks for them, when the day's fast is over, it'll be a real pleasure to sink my teeth into them."

The monkey found ripe mangoes "and thought to himself, we'll see if anyone comes along, otherwise, these will be mine."

The hare, however, was in a state of apprehension. "What can I find? What can I offer? I eat grass, but no one else eats grass."

## The Test

A fairy in the forest heard their pledge and became intrigued. "And she decided to test the confederates to determine the extent of their resolve."

Disguised as a bent, old, emaciated lady, she approached the jackal, then the otter, then the monkey.

"And the monkey, like his friends had secretly hoped that no one would ask for what he had, but when he was asked, he was quite willing to offer it up..."

In each case, the friend willingly offered their findings, and in each case, the fairy declined and moved on.

## The Ultimate Offering

Finally, she came to the hare, who had nothing to give and was utterly distraught.

"And suddenly an idea flashed in his mind. I have nothing to give, but let me just give myself."

He asked the old crone to make a fire. She caused a fire to leap up. "And the hare smiled and without any hesitation, leaped into the fire to be cooked, to be offered as food for the crone."

But something strange occurred: "there was no blistering, there was no burning, there was no singeing of fur. The flames were cool."

The fairy revealed herself. "Oh, hare, I never meant to eat you. I was just playing a game, but your heart is so pure, so generous, so kind."

She then lifted her wand to the moon and inscribed a shape. "And the hare looked up and saw his own portrait on the moon."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Hare's Ultimate Sacrifice
snippets.body:
# The Hare's Ultimate Sacrifice

## The Gift of Self

When the fairy, disguised as an old crone, came to the hare, he was distraught. He had nothing to give.

"Suddenly an idea flashed in his mind. I have nothing to give, but let me just give myself."

He asked the crone to make a fire. She caused one to leap up.

"And the hare smiled and without any hesitation, leaped into the fire to be cooked, to be offered as food for the crone."

But a miracle occurred--there was no burning, no blistering, no singeing of fur.

"The flames were cool. As the flames that enwrapped Abraham proved cool when Abraham was in a garden in the flames."

## The Portrait on the Moon

The fairy revealed her true form and spoke to the hare with a smile.

"Oh, hare, I never meant to eat you. I was just playing a game, but your heart is so pure, so generous, so kind."

Then the fairy directed her wand to a nearby hill, drawing its telluric magnetism into her wand. She lifted it to the moon and inscribed the hare's portrait.

"And that's why when you go out to do your Shughl-i-Mahtabi at night on a clear night and you look up at the moon, you just might see a hare gazing down at you."

## The Alchemy of Self-Giving

This story is a celebration of generosity--"particularly the willingness to help someone in their hour of need, even if it means personal sacrifice."

There is a deeper, alchemical meaning in the fairy's actions.

"The fairy draws energy from the earth into her staff, and then uses that energy to etch a depiction of the face of the being to be hallowed in the moon, which symbolizes the soul. So the wand is like the central axis, and the axis is a link between celestial and terrestrial conditions."

"And through this alchemy of self-love and self-giving and the receipt of that kindness, something of our earthly incarnate condition is essentialized and abstracted so that it ornaments the immaterial life of the soul."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Mind as an Accommodation of the World
snippets.body:
# The Mind as an Accommodation of the World

## The Mind Surrounds the World

"All that exists, whether it is business or anything else, all that is visible or invisible, seems to be outside, but in reality, it is in our mind."

"It is outside because our eyes see it outside, but it is within us because the mind surrounds it. It is accommodated in our mind. Mind is an accommodation of the world which is outside."

The mind isn't smaller than the body--the mind is larger than the body.

"The body sits inside the mind. And whatever is perceived sits within the mind."

## A Heart Larger Than the Universe

"A Hindustani poet describes this wonderfully. The land and sea are not too large for the heart of man to accommodate."

"In other words, the heart of man is larger than the universe. If there were a thousand universes, the human heart could accommodate them."

## The Impression of Limitation

"But we human beings, unaware of our inner being, impressed by outer limitation, remain under the impression of weakness, limitation, smallness."

"And that keeps one from using this great power, which one can find within oneself. This great light with which one can see life more clearly. It is only because one is unaware of oneself."

## A Reorientation for Contemplation

This realization encourages a reorientation away from our ordinary perception.

"Instead of thinking of ourself as a bounded self situated in a larger world, a world through which we move, instead, one comes to experience the body as a figure moving within the field of consciousness, which subsumes everything you perceive."

"As we make our way into the domain of contemplation, we come to more and more identify with consciousness itself and all that it encompasses."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Reorienting Perception Beyond Subject and Object
snippets.body:
# Reorienting Perception Beyond Subject and Object

## The Meaning of 'Inner'

We must be careful with the meaning we ascribe to language. In the inner life, does "turning within" mean that consciousness is located somewhere inside the body?

"No. By inner is meant a shift from the explicit to the implicit, from the obvious to the subtle. This shift doesn't take place spatially."

The mind is not a thing that sits inside the body; it is not smaller than the body. The mind is larger than the body.

"The body sits inside the mind. And whatever is perceived sits within the mind. So the mind pervades."

## A Reorientation for Contemplation

This understanding encourages a fundamental reorientation. We move from the sense of a limited self in a vast world to an entirely different experience.

"Instead of thinking of ourself as a bounded self situated in a larger world, a world through which we move, instead, one comes to experience the body as a figure moving within the field of consciousness, which subsumes everything you perceive."

## A Unified Field of Perception

This realization takes us beyond the reductionist view of a subject confronting an object.

"This universe is made of the overlapping perceptions of all perceiving beings, all of which are waves of the perception of the one ultimate perceiver."

In this unified field, the division between seer and seen dissolves. "Subject and object participate in a unified manner in all that is perceived."

As we deepen our practice, we come to identify more and more with consciousness itself and all that it encompasses.

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Musical Contemplation with Clair de Lune
snippets.body:
# Musical Contemplation with Clair de Lune

"Shall we enter another landscape, another visionary landscape through a musical contemplation?"

This practice uses a vignette from Pir Vilayat Inayat Khan and the music of Debussy's "Clair de Lune" to enter a state of deep contemplation.

## The Visionary Landscape

"Imagine that you are wandering leisurely alongside a tranquil lake. So relieved to have escaped from the humdrum turmoil of life in our modern societies."

"The atmosphere is windless, nebulous, sedate, and eerie under the diaphanous glow of the full moon."

"The sheer sight of the stars peering through evanescent clouds confers a sense of immensity to the environment. The turbulence in your mind and emotions is becalmed. So you cannot resist the quiescence enveloping you. A sigh of relief infuses your being."

## The Practice

1. Close your eyes and imagine yourself walking in this landscape.
2. The object of contemplation is the drizzling rain upon the lake.
3. "As you watch the eddies formed by each drop of water as it impinges on the surface of the lake... you lose yourself in the elaborate wave interference patterns that they describe."
4. Observe them "as they merge and compose, dancing in covert collusion, glistening under the glary eye of the mystic full moon."
5. Continue this contemplation for the duration of the music.

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Shaping Your Hereafter in the Now
snippets.body:
# Shaping Your Hereafter in the Now

## The Intersection of Worlds

After returning from a visionary contemplation, we find that we don't have to exist entirely in one world or the other.

"You might keep one foot as it were in the gossamer landscape of moonlight and shimmering water, finding yourself at the intersection between two worlds, visionary and material."

## The Garden of the Hereafter is Growing Now

Once a trail has been blazed into an inner landscape, it becomes easier to return.

"Having blazed a trail in that scene... you might find that it isn't difficult to go back there... simply by recollecting the state of being that is the key to that particular space."

This ability to travel inwardly by attuning our consciousness is the very method by which we shape our existence beyond this life.

"In fact, you can go anywhere in the universe by attuning to the nature of that place. And our hereafter will be the place that we've chosen for ourself by tuning our mind to it. So if we're going to be in a garden in the hereafter, that garden is already growing now."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Lataif Polarity of Innocence and Experience
snippets.body:
# The Lataif Polarity of Innocence and Experience

A key dichotomy within the Lataif system is the juxtaposition of innocence and experience, represented by the soul (Ruh) on the right side and the heart (Qalb) on the left.

## The Soul (Ruh) — Innocence

"On the right, the soul, which is innocent."

This center is distinct from the sagely self, which is "seasoned by experience." The soul, in contrast, remains untouched by the world.

"The soul is never seasoned in itself. The Ruh is simply the Ruh. It's Atman, it's sheer, luminous awareness. So we discern that capacity through the right side."

## The Heart (Qalb) — Experience

The countervailing side is the heart. "If this is being, then becoming."

The heart is the center for "the process of experiencing life, empathizing, meeting fellow human beings and fellow beings generally in all kinds of vicissitudes and transformations of history and of the world, and finding a path of creativity and service... which brims with passion."

"We can't really attribute passion to the Ruh. It's there in potential, but as yet in a very unheated form."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Root of Creaturehood: Latifa Qalabiyya
snippets.body:
# The Root of Creaturehood — Latifa Qalabiyya

One of the fundamental polarities in the landscape of the Lataif is between the root in the tailbone and the crown at the top of the head. The tailbone is the Latifa Qalabiyya.

## The Mold of Being

The name *Qalabiyya* shares a root with *Qalb* (heart) and can mean "the mold." This reflects an ancient belief that the tailbone holds the essential pattern of our physical form.

"It's the idea that the template of the physical body lies in the tailbone. And therefore that the tailbone is the seed of the body of resurrection, which is based on the physical body."

## Our Crude Creaturehood

"Sufis sometimes called it the crude creaturehood of a person."

This term is not derogatory. From the perspective of Tawhid, or oneness, the manifest and unmanifest are equal, and our physical incarnation is a sacred process.

"In Tawhid, in oneness, there is an equality between the visible and the invisible, the manifest and the unmanifest. We don't differentiate."

"So our crude creaturehood is intrinsic to the sacred process of our bearing witness to the divine treasure. And our incarnation has its role to play in this."

## A Taproot to the Planet's Core

Activating the awareness of the tailbone brings a profound and stabilizing quality of groundedness. It cultivates the quality of being deeply rooted, like a tree.

"There's a taproot that runs right down to the center of the planet."

This center connects us to the very foundation of our world.

"One can hardly imagine how marvelous is the domain beneath the earth's crust, the mineral and metallic and crystalline and magmic worlds underground leading to the planetary core. All of this is the foundation of the tailbone."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Bridge Between Earth and Eternity
snippets.body:
# The Bridge Between Earth and Eternity

The final polarity in the Lataif system is between the root and the crown.

"And then there's one more polarity. And that is between the the root in the tailbone and the crown at the top of the head."

These are not different places, but different modes of perceiving the same reality. "When we go through these doors of the Lataif, we're not going to different places. It's all the same place, just perceived differently."

## The Two Poles

**The Root** — the Latifa Qalabiyya at the tailbone. This is our connection to the earth, our "crude creaturehood."

"There's a taproot that runs right down to the center of the planet. And one can hardly imagine how marvelous is the domain beneath the earth's crust, the mineral and metallic and crystalline and magmic worlds underground leading to the planetary core. All of this is the foundation of the tailbone."

**The Crown** — the Latifa Akhfaiyya at the top of the head.

"Akhfa is most hidden, superlative, profoundly hidden. Why is it hidden? Because it transcends physical sensation... It's the same reality, but experienced in its transcendence, experienced beyond the frame of time and space."

## The Human Alif

We stand between these two poles, embodying the vertical axis that connects them.

"We find ourselves as an alif, its lower extremity rooted in the solidity of the earth, its upper pole lost in eternity. And the spine is the vertical bridge between these two contraries."

This is the ladder of Jacob, which exists within each of us. "Jacob, peace be upon him, discovered this when he ascended and descended along the the ladder that was shown to him... this ladder has been given to all of us, the ladder between the tailbone and the crown."

## Our Birthright to Migrate

It is our purpose to move freely along this inner axis, experiencing both the immanent and the transcendent.

"There are many ways to experience this movement toward transcendence. And there are many ways to experience the contrary movement toward incarnation."

"To be able to move back and forth between transcendence and immanence, between the crystalline planet and the... beyond the beyond, the ocean beyond the ocean... the stark mystery in the face of which even the deepest blackness pales, to be able to migrate, to move to and fro, this is our this is our birthright."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Forest Fairy Saka and the Unsuria
snippets.body:
# The Forest Fairy Saka and the Unsuria

## Guardian Spirits of Nature

"Many places in nature have guardian spirits."

"These are, these are Unsuria, these are earthly jinn, and sometimes hybrid jinn who occupy landscapes and who protect them in various ways or simply personify the energy of the place."

## The Test of Resolve

The fairy in this story, whose name is Saka, overheard the friends make their pledge the night before.

"And so she became very intrigued. She wanted to see what would happen. And she decided to to, uh, test the confederates to determine the extent of their resolve."

"And so she disguised herself as a bent old, emaciated lady and hobbled around."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Telluric Magnetism and Shughl-i-Mahtabi
snippets.body:
# Telluric Magnetism and Shughl-i-Mahtabi

## The Hare in the Moon

"The fairy directed her wand to a nearby hill, and the telluric magnetism of the hill poured out from the hill into her wand. And then she lifted the wand to the moon and inscribed a shape. And the hare looked up and saw his own portrait on the moon."

"And that's why when you go out to do your Shughl-i-Mahtabi at night on a clear night and you look up at the moon, you just might see a hare gazing down at you."

## An Alchemy of Self-Giving

"There's a detail in the story that's quite remarkable. No doubt you noticed how the fairy draws energy from the earth into her staff, and then uses that energy to etch a depiction of the face of the being to be hallowed in the moon, which symbolizes the soul."

"So the wand is like the central axis, and the axis is a link between celestial and terrestrial conditions."

"And through this alchemy of self-love and self-giving and the receipt of that kindness, something of our earthly incarnate condition is essentialized and abstracted so that it ornaments the immaterial life of the soul."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Noor Inayat Khan's Sacrifice and the Jataka Tale
snippets.body:
# Noor Inayat Khan's Sacrifice and the Jataka Tale

## The Alchemy of Self-Giving

"And through this alchemy of self-love and self-giving and the receipt of that kindness, something of our earthly incarnate condition is essentialized and abstracted so that it ornaments the immaterial life of the soul."

"Beautiful story. It was, I believe it was the favorite of the Jataka tales that Noor told. It was her her personal favorite."

## Noor's Own Sacrifice

"And tragically, as you know, she went into the fire in the end herself, and that was a consequence of her self-giving for the sake of those in need."

"And we can be sure that her insignia is emblazoned in the heavens."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Review of the Nafs and Sage Polarity
snippets.body:
# The Polarity of the Nafs and the Sage

A review of the first polarity in the Lataif system—the juxtaposition of the socially conditioned self and the serene, far-seeing sage within.

## The Nafs — The Valley Below

We begin with the center in the belly, the one most salient to our conditioned existence.

"We began with the the center that is perhaps more, most salient to our day-to-day social state of being. So much so that for many people, it's most of what one knows... it's kind of home base."

This is the socially situated self, reacting to circumstances.

## The Sage — The Mountain Top

In contrast, there is a parallel center that offers a higher vantage point.

"There's a parallel center that, um, is to the Nafs what the mountain top is to the valley below, a higher vantage point, a place of farther vision."

This is the sage in you, a dimension accessed in moments of profound peace.

"There have been moments when you rise above the the fray of life and taste a certain peace and satisfaction, a calm that isn't disturbed by urgent longings. It's the the sage in you."

This quality often predominates in the later years of life, which is one reason we honor our elders.

"It tends to predominate in the latter years of life, particularly, the golden years of a life when one has one by one put aside the things that one has occupied oneself with and one is able to just be, to be a presence."

"That's why we honor our elders, because we sense in them that that station."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Nasama, the Ethereal Body, and the Ghost Limb
snippets.body:
# The Nasama, the Ethereal Body, and the Ghost Limb

## The Energetic Mold and the Ghost Limb

"It may not be that this body is built up solely upon the model of what is in the genome... but that there's an underlying energetic mold, which you can actually feel even in cases where, let's say a limb is missing."

"Captain Ahab in Moby Dick lost a leg but could still feel the leg. It's an example. It's called a ghost limb."

## The Template of Our Physicality

"It's the principle that there is an underlying matrix that is the template of our physicality, and that it's the basis of regeneration--one basis of regeneration, the way that amphibians can regrow tails, for instance."

"And also it is rooted in the earth itself... and that rootedness functions primarily through the tailbone."

## Sacred Creaturehood

"Sufis sometimes called it the crude creaturehood of a person. That could sound derogatory, but... in Tawhid, in oneness, there is an equality between the visible and the invisible, the manifest and the unmanifest."

"Our crude creaturehood is intrinsic to the sacred process of our bearing witness to the divine treasure. And our incarnation has its role to play in this."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Hidden and Most Hidden: Khafi and Akhfa
snippets.body:
# The Hidden and Most Hidden: Khafi and Akhfa

At the crown of the head is the Latifa Akhfaiyya. It shares an etymology with the Latifa Khafi in the forehead, revealing a gradation of subtlety.

"Khafi in the forehead is hidden, and Akhfa is most hidden, superlative, profoundly hidden."

## Why It Is Most Hidden

The reason this center is considered "most hidden" is that it lies beyond our primary mode of knowing.

"Why is it hidden? Because it transcends physical sensation, and we mostly know life through our senses."

"It doesn't mean it's far away or unrelated to us or irrelevant. It's profoundly present, but it's not different from the manifest. It's the same reality, but experienced in its transcendence, experienced beyond the frame of time and space."

## The Same Place, Perceived Differently

Accessing this center is not a matter of going to a different place, but of perceiving the same reality in a different way.

"We have to remember that these aren't, when we go through these doors of the Lataif, we're not going to different places. It's all the same place, just perceived differently. And in the degree of Akhfa, it's perceived without any temporal container."

"So it's natural that we associate transcendence with the heavens because in our direct experience, the earth under our feet is solid and opaque, whereas the sky is translucent and the night sky is evidently infinite."

## The Alif of Being

This polarity between the earthly and the transcendent is bridged by our own being.

"So we find ourselves as an alif, its lower extremity rooted in the solidity of the earth, its upper pole lost in eternity. And the spine is the vertical bridge between these two contraries."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Tasawwul Zikr Practice on Jacob's Ladder
snippets.body:
# The Tasawwul Zikr Practice on Jacob's Ladder

## The Ladder Within

"The spine is the vertical bridge between these two contraries... this ladder has been given to all of us, the ladder between the tailbone and the crown."

"To be able to migrate, to move to and fro, this is our this is our birthright."

## The Tasawwul Practice

"We're moving on this ladder when we say the syllables of the Tasawwul practice."

1. Start with the "Ha," which moves up from the tailbone to the chest.
2. Then go into the "Hu," which is from the chest up to the forehead.
3. Say this a few times: "Ha. Hu. Ha. Hu. Ha. Hu."

## The Two Poles of Being

"Tailbone is rooted in the center of the earth. Crown is open to the infinite."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Interplay of Inner and Outer Worlds
snippets.body:
# The Interplay of Inner and Outer Worlds

## Different Modes of Awareness

"The outside world is not only outside, and the world within is not only within."

"At night when we close our eyes and go to sleep, what seemed to be the outside world enters us and becomes the world within. And when we wake up in the morning and remember our dreams and form our intentions for the day, the world within radiates out and fills the space that surrounds us."

This realization leads to a new understanding of perception. "Within and without are not different places, they're different modes of awareness."

## A Fikr Practice

This understanding can be practiced as a Fikr, a prayerful remembrance of the divine presence in both realms.

1.  **On the exhale:** "Be thou before me when I am awake"
2.  **On the inhale:** "and within me when I am asleep"

## The Intention of the Prayer

"The greatest prayer is when that which we ask is the one from whom we ask it. I don't ask anything from you, beloved, but your own being. I ask to be with you."

This practice is an affirmation of our intention to perceive the divine in all states. "Whether we look through physical senses or subtler senses, the prayer is, may these senses meet you."

"So when we say this prayer, it isn't really so much to ask for something that as of yet isn't here, but the prayer signifies our own intention to meet what is here, to wake up to it, to be present."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: A Fikr Practice for Divine Presence
snippets.body:
# A Fikr Practice for Divine Presence

A prayerful Fikr (remembrance) to realize the divine presence that is already here, whether our senses are turned outward to the world or inward to the states of sleep and dream.

## A Prayer to Meet What Is Here

"Fa-aynama tuwallu fa-thamma wajhu-Llah. Wheresoever you turn, there is the divine face."

"When we say this prayer, it isn't really so much to ask for something that as of yet isn't here, but the prayer signifies our own intention to meet what is here, to wake up to it, to be present."

"And really all prayers are like this, if one looks deeply into them. Whenever we lift up our hands and ask for blessings, in the act of asking, we are turning our attention to the blessing that is already here for us. The imagined future is realized in the prayerful present."

## The Practice

1. As you exhale, say the words: "Be thou before me when I am awake."
2. As you inhale, say the words: "and within me when I am asleep."
3. Practice first with spoken words, then move to reciting them silently within.
4. Finally, "let the words dissolve away. So what remains is simply the intention."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: The Highest Form of Prayer
snippets.body:
# The Highest Form of Prayer

## The Greatest Prayer

"There are prayers in which we ask for something we wish for from the one whom we ask it from. But then the greatest prayer is when that which we ask is the one from whom we ask it."

This is a prayer that turns from all lesser requests to the source of all blessing itself.

"I don't ask anything from you, beloved, but your own being. I ask to be with you."

"I ask to be face to face with you. I ask to live in the light of your presence."

## A Practice of Presence

This prayer can be practiced as a Fikr, a remembrance with the breath.

1.  As you exhale, say the words: "Be thou before me when I am awake."
2.  As you inhale, say the words: "and within me when I am asleep."

"Whether we look through physical senses or subtler senses, the prayer is, may these senses meet you."

## Waking Up to What Is Here

The meeting we pray for is already a reality.

"Fa-aynama tuwallu fa-thamma wajhu-Llah. Wheresoever you turn, there is the divine face."

"So when we say this prayer, it isn't really so much to ask for something that as of yet isn't here, but the prayer signifies our own intention to meet what is here, to wake up to it, to be present."

This is the deeper truth of all prayer. "Whenever we lift up our hands and ask for blessings, in the act of asking, we are turning our attention to the blessing that is already here for us. The imagined future is realized in the prayerful present."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*

snippets.title: Walking in a Living Tricosm
snippets.body:
# Walking in a Living Tricosm

## The World as a Tricosm

The practice of contemplative walking begins with a shift in perception.

"Discern the world around you not merely as a flat land devoid of spirit, but instead as a tricosm, brimming not only with matter but also with psychic life and spiritual life, in all manner of manifestations."

As you walk, be aware of the multiple dimensions of your being and your world, all present at once.

"Walking, aware of your breath, aware of your heartbeat, sensing the etheric body within your physical body and the psychic double at the same time and the the soul itself, all of these dimensions are simultaneous as you're walking."

"You're not alone. Innumerable beings surround you... The sun is shining down and all the stars are pouring their light down over the earth and into your flesh and blood and bones. Gravity is gently holding you to the earth. Gravity which itself is a phenomenon of Ishq."

## Servant of the Name

Into this practice, we introduce the sacred transmission of a divine name.
